skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Guides debugging through root cause investigation, pattern comparison, hypothesis testing, and focused implementation before attempting fixes.
skill.summary: Debug bugs and test failures with a disciplined root cause workflow.
skill.author: DMJGilbert

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed against the single skill file. The two weak cryptography detections are false positives in normal prose, and the external command detection is a benign suggestion to inspect local git changes.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection in Description
low_findings.description: The static detector flagged SKILL.md line 3 as a weak cryptographic algorithm, but the line is plain metadata describing root cause investigation. No cryptographic API, algorithm choice, or executable code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line contains only the skill description. There is no evidence of cryptographic functionality or unsafe algorithm use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Mention
low_findings.description: The static detector flagged SKILL.md line 24 because it mentions `git diff`. This is guidance for a user or agent to inspect local repository changes, not embedded command execution or shell scripting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command appears inside instructional Markdown and is not executed by the skill. The guidance is narrowly related to debugging recent changes.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection in Checklist
low_findings.description: The static detector flagged SKILL.md line 69 as a weak cryptographic algorithm, but the line asks users to question whether a design is sound. No security-sensitive cryptographic operation exists there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line is debugging process guidance. There is no code, dependency, or configuration related to cryptography.

content.value_statement: Bug fixes often fail when teams patch symptoms before understanding causes. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured investigation workflow before implementation.
actual_capabilities: Guides a four-phase debugging process from investigation to implementation.
Prompts agents to reproduce failures before proposing fixes.
Encourages comparison with similar working code in the same codebase.
Requires explicit hypotheses and small tests before changing code.
Defines stopping rules when repeated fixes fail.
limitations: Does not run diagnostics or tests by itself.
Does not provide language-specific debugging commands.
Depends on available error messages, logs, and reproducible examples.
May slow simple fixes when the root cause is already obvious.

use_cases.title: Investigate failing tests
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to reproduce a test failure, inspect recent changes, and isolate the root cause before editing code.

use_cases.title: Review unstable bug fixes
use_cases.description: Apply the checklist when several attempted fixes have failed and the team needs to reset the investigation.

use_cases.title: Guide AI coding sessions
use_cases.description: Ask an AI assistant to follow the phases so it gathers evidence before suggesting implementation changes.

prompt_templates.title: Start a bug investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging to investigate this bug. First restate the failure, identify the evidence needed, and list reproduction steps before proposing any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a test failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging on this failing test. Read the error, compare it with similar passing tests, and state a root cause hypothesis with evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Recover after failed fixes
prompt_templates.prompt: Three fixes have failed. Use systematic-debugging to stop implementation, reassess the architecture, and decide whether the pattern should be changed.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a verified repair
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging to create a minimal failing test, propose one focused fix for the confirmed root cause, and define verification steps.

output_examples.input: A login test started failing after a dependency update.
output_examples.output: The assistant would summarize the failure, inspect recent dependency and configuration changes, compare passing login paths, and state a testable hypothesis before editing.

output_examples.input: A production bug persists after two quick patches.
output_examples.output: The assistant would pause further fixes, trace the bad value backward, identify assumptions, and recommend a single evidence-backed change.

output_examples.input: A refactor caused intermittent behavior in a service.
output_examples.output: The assistant would look for a reliable reproduction, add boundary diagnostics, compare working service patterns, and separate architecture concerns from symptoms.

best_practices: Provide complete errors, logs, and reproduction steps before asking for fixes.
Let the assistant inspect similar working code before implementation.
Ask for one hypothesis and one verification step at a time.
anti_patterns: Requesting quick patches before the failure is understood.
Changing several variables during one debugging attempt.
Continuing random fixes after repeated failures.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants debug bugs, failing tests, and unexpected behavior using a structured root cause process.

faq.question: Does it execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown instruction skill. It may suggest inspecting local changes as part of debugging.

faq.question: Can it replace tests?
faq.answer: No. It encourages failing tests and verification, but users must run or approve the actual tests.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when a bug is unclear, a test fails, behavior changed unexpectedly, or prior fixes did not work.

faq.question: Is it specific to one programming language?
faq.answer: No. The workflow is language independent and focuses on investigation, evidence, and verification.

faq.question: Why does it tell the assistant to stop after failed fixes?
faq.answer: Repeated failed fixes often mean the root cause or design assumption is wrong. The skill redirects attention to evidence.
